data.title: Work in progress: Hong Kong reaps the fruits of fintech seeds sown nearly 10 years ago
data.description:
When Alvin Kwock, co-founder of digital insurer OneDegree, took part in the inaugural Hong Kong FinTech Week in November 2016, it was held at a venue suitable for only a few hundred people.
“As it was the first ever fintech event in Hong Kong, only fintech start-ups were interested, no big financial institutions paid attention,” Kwock said. “People did not have a clear idea of fintech as it was still in an exploratory stage.”
In the years since, fintech in Hong Kong has gone through a sea change. “Now fintech is no longer confined to a few start-ups; it’s on the top of the agenda of banks and insurers,” Kwock said.
The venue has moved from the PMQ (formerly the Police Married Quarters) to the massive Hong Kong Convention and Exhibition Centre, with the number of attendees growing 18-fold to 45,000 people. At the recent forum, speakers included some of the most high-profile figures in the financial sector like HSBC CEO Georges Elhedery and Standard Chartered Bank CEO Bill Winters.

This maturation has intensified Hong Kong’s long-standing rivalry with Singapore. The two cities are competing to develop fintech, a rivalry highlighted this month as both hosted their 10th annual flagship fintech festivals.
While attendance in Hong Kong may have been thinner than in Singapore, the city has an edge over its Southeast Asian rival, thanks to its vibrant capital market providing funding for fintech companies and serving as a gateway to the Greater Bay Area.
Hong Kong secured the world’s top fintech ranking for the first time in the Global Financial Centres Index in September, while Singapore ranked fourth, according to the report by Z/Yen and the City of London that tracked 116 centres based on criteria like regulations, access to finance and talent.
In the inaugural survey on fintech in 2017, Hong Kong ranked seventh and Singapore eighth. The same year, the Hong Kong Monetary Authority (HKMA) introduced the first phase of fintech development.


Since then, the HKMA has issued eight digital banking licences, with the lenders offering online-only services. This week, British digital bank Revolut announced plans to launch operations in Hong Kong.
The arrival of global players is a direct result of a concerted regulatory push – the HKMA’s fintech initiatives, the Insurance Authority’s promotion of virtual insurance, and the Securities and Futures Commission’s rules for virtual asset trading. Bourse operator Hong Kong Exchanges and Clearing’s listing reforms too have paved the way for start-ups to raise funds.
“Hong Kong’s fintech development in the past decade has been nothing short of transformative,” said HKMA chief executive Eddie Yue Wai-man in a written interview. The 18-fold increase in the number of participants in this year’s FinTech Week compared with nine years ago was “proof not only of growing interest but also of a hub that has become a magnet for talent, ideas and ambition in fintech”, he said.
However, a broader measure of digital competitiveness reveals ongoing challenges. Despite Hong Kong’s many advantages, Singapore ranked third and Hong Kong fourth in the 2025 World Digital Competitiveness Ranking released earlier this month.


The report, however, noted that Hong Kong performed the worst in “IT integration”, placing 29th overall, due to low scores in government cybersecurity capacity and legal framework for privacy protection.
Singapore, which topped the ranking in 2024, fell behind Switzerland and the US this year.
The report said Singapore was strong in its digital regulatory framework and hi-tech patent grants, topping these categories. The institute also highlighted Singapore’s weakness in two areas, where it ranked 29th in technology, media and telecommunications stock market capitalisation and 61st in telecommunications investment.
Analysts and fintech firms regard Singapore and Hong Kong as Asian fintech leaders, albeit with different roles.
“Singapore offers a more accessible pathway for regional expansion across Asean, whereas Hong Kong’s competitive strength remains closely tied to the China-Hong Kong corridor and its leadership in regulated digital-asset innovation,” said Benjamin Quinlan, CEO and managing partner of Quinlan & Associates, a Hong Kong-based consultancy.
Quinlan said Hong Kong had also made progress in digital asset and tokenisation initiatives, including the coming stablecoin regime and tokenised government bond issuance, which provided a unique competitive angle in this emerging sector.


Singapore, by contrast, had positioned itself as a launch pad for the Association of Southeast Asian Nations, while the country had a clean regulatory environment and was innovation-friendly, supported by comparatively lower barriers to entry and a strong investor appetite, Quinlan said.
The HKMA’s Yue said the gateway role to mainland China would continue to be a major advantage for Hong Kong’s fintech development, as companies took advantage of cross-border trading schemes in stocks, bonds, swaps, exchange-traded products and wealth management.
Yue added that the development of fintech had benefited many sectors like digital banks, while also making it easier for small and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs) to get bank loans via the Commercial Data Interchange (CDI).
The CDI, which was launched in October 2022 as part of the HKMA’s Fintech 2025 strategy, is a financial data platform designed to make it easier for businesses, especially SMEs, to access financial services. The CDI had facilitated over 71,000 loan applications totalling HK$58.1 billion (US$7.5 billion) for SMEs until the end of September.
“Think of a wonton noodle shop that could use the CDI to share its historical turnover data from point-of-sale terminals with a bank, which could then use this alternative data for a more accurate credit assessment and loan approval, all without requiring traditional collateral,” Yue said. “We are also advancing trade finance digitalisation through CargoX to support SME traders in a complex global trade landscape.”
Project CargoX is a public-private collaboration led by the HKMA to use cargo and trade data to improve trade finance.

One of the HKMA’s first fintech initiatives was the launch of the Faster Payment System in 2018, with the platform currently boasting more than 18 million users. This was followed by licences for eight digital banks in 2019.
Even in a market with more than 150 lenders, these virtual banks had 3.4 million customers, with HK$77 billion in deposits and HK$29 billion in loans as of June, according to the HKMA.
To build on this decade of progress and solidify Hong Kong’s future position, Yue unveiled the Fintech 2030 road map this month. The five-year programme will launch more than 40 initiatives in four areas: data, artificial intelligence, resilience and tokenisation.
The city has also incubated some prominent fintech unicorns – start-ups with a valuation of at least US$1 billion – like Airwallex, HashKey Group, WeLab, Micro Connect and ZA Group.
Hong Kong currently has more than 1,200 fintech companies, a 10 per cent increase from last year, with the sector’s revenue expected to exceed US$606 billion by 2032, according to a recent report by the Financial Services Development Council.
The UK’s Revolut will add to the tally.
Revolut is a global disrupter with over 65 million clients and has a strong presence in the US, Europe and Asia, so adding Hong Kong was the next stop in the expansion, said Camilo Ramirez, head of financial services at Sia Partners Hong Kong, a consultancy.
“While Hong Kong looks like a saturated market, it still lags behind in digital capabilities compared with Europe and mainland China,” Ramirez said. “With the HKMA launching the fintech road map, Revolut would land in a perfect environment to bring its innovative capabilities to Hong Kong at a pivotal and exciting moment for the financial services industry.”

One Degree’s Kwock said the conversations around fintech had continued to evolve at FinTech Week. Initially, it was about exploring the use of fintech, which later shifted to digital banks, digital insurance and virtual assets trading, before moving to AI this year, he said.
“Cybersecurity will be a key issue going forward as the city becomes a virtual-asset trading hub,” Kwock said.
Meanwhile, home-grown fintechs like WeLab have been using their early-mover advantage to good use by expanding in Southeast Asia.
WeLab serves 70 million customers via its WeLend online lending platform and two digital banks in Hong Kong and Indonesia, WeLab Bank and Bank Saqu, respectively.
“Positioned at the nexus of two major growth corridors – northbound to the Greater Bay Area and mainland China, and southbound to the rapidly growing and digitally transformative markets of Southeast Asia, Hong Kong acts as a natural springboard for regional fintech expansion,” said Simon Loong, founder and group CEO of WeLab.
Fintech has also transformed one of Hong Kong’s oldest banks. The 160-year-old HSBC has seen an increase in digital use across all banking transactions.
“We are already a fully digital bank, prioritising digital channels to deliver daily banking services and engage with our customers,” said Maggie Ng, CEO and head of retail banking and wealth at HSBC Hong Kong.
Ng said HSBC would take part in the HKMA’s Fintech 2030 projects. The bank had already introduced tokenised deposits for customers, and its digital asset platform, HSBC Orion, was used to launch the world’s largest digital bond issuance by the Hong Kong government this month, she said.
The bank recently launched HSBC Gold Token, a tokenised gold product for retail customers in Hong Kong.
Also this month, Hong Kong and Shenzhen launched a joint fintech action plan to promote cross-boundary financial innovation, setting a target of establishing over 20 fintech application scenarios by the end of 2027. Secretary for Financial Services and the Treasury Christopher Hui Ching-yu, who signed the initial pact with Shenzhen, said the two sides would implement various initiatives to facilitate high-quality fintech development.
Paul Sin, chair of the web3 and technology committee for Greater China at CPA Australia, an accounting industry body, said Hong Kong’s Fintech 2030 initiative provided a clear road map for the next five years.
One element that Hong Kong needed to develop was a decentralised identity (DID) system, which would allow financial firms to easily verify users’ identities.
“Only when firms can easily verify clients’ identities using technology like DID can fintech solutions be both innovative and compliant,” Sin said.

data.title: Shanghai bets on manufacturing to buttress finance in two-pronged growth plan
data.description:
Shanghai, mainland China’s top finance and trade hub, is set to double down on efforts to prevent the “hollowing out” of its manufacturing sector even as it pursues global financial centre status.
The city’s next five-year plan, which will cover the period from 2026 to 2030, is expected to contain measures designed to lift manufacturing’s share of gross domestic product to no less than 25 per cent, in line with a national imperative to bolster the real economy and promote cutting-edge technology.
The move would make Shanghai one of the first major Chinese cities to include a specific goal for stabilising the manufacturing sector in its next five-year plan, and would run concurrently with a broader campaign to win the city recognition as a fully-fledged global financial centre by 2035.
The latter goal was reaffirmed in a document released in June by the Communist Party’s Central Financial Commission, a party organ headed by Premier Li Qiang that oversees China’s vast financial holdings.
“Shanghai officials are resolved to avoid the fate of economic hollowing out and the exodus of industrial enterprises, a phenomenon prevalent among some global cities as they develop into international financial hubs,” said Fu Weigang, president of the Shanghai Institute of Finance and Law think tank.
“Shanghai aims to have two legs of balanced growth: a competitive services sector like finance plus advanced manufacturing, to make the economy more resilient.
“It’s more like learning from the lessons of Hong Kong, New York and others.”


Shanghai’s 25 per cent goal, first broached for discussion in official policy papers in 2016, was also reiterated in a three-year action plan gazetted last year on the building of a complete and modern manufacturing ecosystem – something lacking in Hong Kong and New York.
China’s top leadership has also pledged to prevent industrial hollowing out by putting a floor under manufacturing’s declining share of economic output.
But in an outline of official priorities for the next five years, which offered a detailed interpretation of proposals for the next national five-year plan, top policymakers admitted it was a trend that was difficult to reverse.
The Communist Party’s Central Committee unveiled its proposals for the 2026-2030 blueprint last month, with a focus on building a modern industrial system by “maintaining a reasonable share of manufacturing”, though it did not set a specific target.
For Shanghai, keeping its factories humming and upgrading production lines remains a tall order.
China’s largest city economy, with GDP hitting 5.39 trillion yuan (US$761 billion) last year, up 5 per cent year on year, is seeing its booming financial sector crowd out industrial producers.
The service sector accounted for 79.1 per cent of the city’s economy in the first half of this year, up from 78.2 per cent last year and an all-time high, leaving the share of manufacturing at around 20 per cent given the city’s negligible agricultural output.
In the first three quarters of this year, Shanghai’s industrial output grew by 3.9 per cent year on year, but that was outpaced by the local economy’s 5.5 per cent overall growth.
China’s national push for tech and economic security for the next five years hinges on key cities like Shanghai
Wang Sizheng, Shanghai Institute of Macroeconomics
But Fu emphasised that manufacturing spanned a wide spectrum in Shanghai, adding the city’s economy was still more diversified and balanced than that of many other metropolises: “Shanghai’s rise was fuelled by its industrial base … Does New York City have a big auto sector? No. Does London have a huge shipbuilding yard or a big refinery? No. Does Hong Kong have an aircraft maker or a big chip foundry? No.”
Automobiles, shipbuilding, petrochemicals, civil aviation, chipmaking and integrated circuits are being touted as the pillars of Shanghai’s manufacturing upgrade. Tesla operates a sprawling factory in Shanghai, and the Commercial Aircraft Corporation of China – maker of the country’s indigenous C919 passenger airliner – and chipmaker SMIC are both headquartered in the city.
Fu said that in contrast to cities that had failed to avert deindustrialisation, Shanghai had an ample supply of land for new industries and also benefited from the government’s iron resolve and systematic planning.
Wang Sizheng, president of the Shanghai Institute of Macroeconomics, told Jiefang Daily, the news organ of the city’s party committee, last month that defending Shanghai’s manufacturing base carried nationwide significance.
“China’s national push for tech and economic security for the next five years hinges on key cities like Shanghai to be on the vanguard of propping up manufacturing,” he said.
Wang, who also sits on a panel advising on the drafting of Shanghai’s new five-year plan, revealed the city would formulate ways to juggle the tasks of nurturing its financial sector while retaining and cultivating industrial enterprises and clusters.
“It’s not just about Shanghai, but rather the city’s responsibility to take the lead nationwide in promoting manufacturing, represent China in global competition and safeguard national industrial security,” he added.

data.title: Why Chinese women like me are done being quiet and ‘appropriate’
data.description:
In China, girls are taught early to be “appropriate” – to listen, not lead; to speak gently, not loudly. We grow up believing that quietness is likeability, and likeability is safety. But something is shifting.
When Chinese writer Jiang Fangzhou said to me during a recent interview, “I can’t be waiting for some influential man to say, ‘I’ll give you an hour to talk about literature’. You can’t be waiting for a seat, for a microphone”, it struck a chord far beyond us. It was a generational declaration – for women who are done being echoes.
Jiang’s trajectory is familiar to many in China. She began writing at seven, published her first book at nine and rose to fame as a newspaper columnist at 12. Once celebrated as a literary prodigy, she was later consumed by controversy, drawing plaudits and attacks in equal measure.
When I first interviewed her a decade ago, she was visibly weary of being labelled a “beautiful female writer”. “I still want to write a satisfying long work,” she said then. “But if I never do, being a ‘young writer’, a ‘middle-aged writer’ and one day, an ‘old writer’ is fine too.”
Back then, few of us – myself included – had the language to talk about gender or identity. Feminism felt abstract, even Western. We didn’t yet see how deeply the idea of female “appropriateness” shaped our behaviour. I mistook restraint for maturity and believed that to be articulate was to risk being disliked.
So we adapted. We learned the rules, fit in and rarely questioned the rules themselves.

After several quiet years away from the internet, Jiang re-emerged this year not as a former prodigy, but as a creator consciously reclaiming her voice. With her new literary podcast, she prepares 10,000-character scripts for every episode, sharing the joy of reading and her own reflections. She has taken back the microphone.
Jiang often cites A Room of One’s Own, the essay in which Virginia Woolf advised that a woman must have five hundred pounds a year and a room of her own to write.
Jiang takes the idea further: “Perhaps a woman shouldn’t just have a room of her own, she should have a house of her own. With a bedroom, a study and, more importantly, a living room – a space for discourse she hosts.”
In our Confucian-rooted patriarchal culture, women are praised for gentleness, not for volume. We learn to nod at the right moment, to speak softly, to never interrupt. Our tone must please; our logic must bend; even our anger must be graceful.
This isn’t weakness – it’s conditioning. From childhood, we’re rewarded for being “understanding”, “cooperative”, “easy to work with”. We internalise those expectations until self-censorship feels like common sense. In meetings, we preface disagreement with polite disclaimers. In social situations, we smooth our edges for harmony.

Over time, we become fluent in quietness. Our voices shrink, our faces blur and our opinions dissolve into politeness.
This obedience isn’t just personal – it’s systemic. It lives in the way teachers praise “sensible girls”, how offices value “team players” and how public opinion punishes women deemed “difficult”. The control imposed on women isn’t loud or violent; it’s subtle and internalised. For years, I believed that to be heard, one had to be agreeable. But silence doesn’t earn respect; it only erases you.
Ten years after our first meeting, when I sat down with Jiang again last month, the atmosphere felt entirely different. Our planned 30-minute interview stretched to 90 minutes; we talked until the camera battery died and day turned to dusk.
She spoke with excitement about how female creators today “finally feel ready to attract their own audiences”. Then she turned to me: “So you should start a podcast too! Did I inspire you?”
Even after the camera stopped rolling, she kept brainstorming: “Make something you love! Maybe a nonfiction series, or reflections on your years studying overseas. Or if that feels too heavy and sensitive – talk about parenting!”

A few days later, she sent me an email: “Looking forward to seeing you again – and I hope you start your podcast soon. I think you’ll be great at it.”
That small message meant more than encouragement. It reflected a quiet cultural shift. Ten years ago, Chinese women often saw each other as rivals; mutual support was rare. Today, even though opportunities are scarcer, competition harsher, and paths narrower, more women are not only speaking up but also passing the microphone along.
“Girls help girls” has become more than a hashtag – it’s a social current born not of abundance, but of scarcity. In a tighter, more unforgiving landscape, solidarity itself becomes an act of defiance.
Since the #MeToo movement, Chinese women have become bolder in public conversation. Across generations, they are speaking openly about marriage, motherhood, workplace bias and emotional freedom. Podcasts, social media and women-led communities have created new spaces for expression and connection.
Woolf once urged women to “write what you wish to write; that is all that matters”. A century later, a new generation of Chinese women are doing exactly that – speaking out, building their own living rooms and refusing to be well-behaved echoes any longer. Perhaps this is the most powerful response to “involution” yet: when crowded race tracks offer little room for growth, women are carving out new squares and inviting each other in.

data.title: China bets on bio-manufacturing as new growth engine, bid for tech self-reliance
data.description:
China is betting on bio-manufacturing to boost technological self-reliance and drive economic growth as part of the next five-year plan, elevating the sector on par with electric vehicles and semiconductors.
Beijing is upgrading and expanding a nationwide bio-ecosystem, with 43 companies and research institutes selected in the first batch of pilot-scale manufacturing platforms for products ranging from biopharmaceuticals to food additives and enzymes to cosmetics, according to a statement from the Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT) earlier this month.
The ministry’s plan highlights the sector’s importance as the country’s next growth engine, analysts said.
“In the coming years, biotech, including innovative drugs, will definitely be a top priority for the country,” said John Yung, managing director and head of Asia healthcare research at Citigroup. “The country needs to grow the whole ecosystem by upgrading manufacturing lines and production capacity, along with expanding clinical management abilities.”

Yang Huang, head of China healthcare research at JPMorgan Chase echoed the view, noting “biological manufacturing has been identified as one of the key economic growth drivers in the government’s 15th five-year plan”.
The pilot-scale platforms are designed to help innovators overcome bottlenecks in research and development, manufacturing and supply chain before achieving commercial-scale production – a phase often called the “valley of death”, as promising innovations can stall.
“In the past, it was difficult for researchers to access pilot-scale manufacturing, as manufacturers were often unwilling to take on small, unprofitable orders,” Huang said. “With these new platforms, researchers may now have a way to move one step closer to potential commercialisation.”
According to a policy document released by the MIIT in June, the initiative highlighted a market-oriented approach, encouraging pilot platforms to provide paid services. It also called for enhancing data security and biosafety capabilities.
By 2027, the government aimed to have pilot-scale platforms serve more than 200 enterprises and convert the research outcomes into more than 400 commercial products, it said.
What mattered the most was the policy guidance and official endorsement for the research institutes and companies on the list, according to Bruce Liu, senior partner at global consulting firm Simon-Kucher.
“Gaining official recognition from the MIIT is itself a form of capital or qualification,” Liu said. “They can now tell their clients that they are part of the first-batch certified pilot platform and they can legitimately charge market rates for their services.”
Local governments were expected to provide additional support through subsidies, tax incentives or special funds for companies and institutes on the list, Liu added.
The government backing comes as Chinese biotechs are enjoying huge success in licensing their early-stage drug candidates to global pharmaceutical giants.
Chinese pharmaceutical companies’ sales of early-stage assets to global partners soared to nearly US$70 billion as of September 2025, an eightfold increase compared with 2021, according to a report by L.E.K. Consulting and PharmaDJ.
The policy aims to sharpen China’s biopharma competitiveness as a growing number of multinational drug developers tap the country’s biotech sector to address drug pricing and patent expiration pressures, analysts said.
About 60 per cent of China’s out-licensing deals for antibody-drug conjugates were powered by WuXi Biologics and WuXi XDC, a leading global contract research, development and manufacturing organisation, said Cui Cui, head of healthcare research for Asia at Jefferies.
A total of 17 entities on the government’s list, scattered across Beijing, the Xinjiang Uygur autonomous region, Jilin, Jiangsu and other provinces and regions, are engaged in biopharmaceutical production. These include Beijing Joinn Laboratories, which is dual-listed in Shanghai and Hong Kong; Shanghai-listed Cabio Biotech Wuhan; Bloomage Biotechnology (Tianjin); and the state-owned Changchun Institute of Biological Products.
Most of the companies on the list are little-known names, according to Citigroup’s Yung. “The top-tier players, like WuXi Bio, don’t need this kind of support,” he said. “As they are already well-established, there is a need to nurture a second tier of players that have both potential and quality.”
China’s demand for contract development and manufacturing services would only grow as the country’s innovative drug pipeline expands, Yung added. “The goal is not just to serve domestic innovators but also to attract international clients.”
JPMorgan’s Huang said most Chinese biotech companies would continue to rely on established contract research organisations and manufacturers for support.
“These new platforms could introduce more competition for very early-stage projects, providing additional manufacturing options for novel drug developers, especially those with limited funding,” he added.
Shanghai, one of the key hubs in this initiative, has designated synthetic biology as a priority area, according to state news agency Xinhua. The city aims to incubate at least five top-tier scientific teams by the end of this year.
In the latest list of newly elected academics released by the Chinese Academy of Sciences, the country’s top institution for science and technology, 32 scholars from life sciences-related fields accounted for more than a third of all new members.

data.title: DeepSeek releases first open AI model with gold-level scores at maths olympiad
data.description:
Chinese artificial intelligence company DeepSeek has released the world’s first open AI model to test and score a gold medal-level performance at the annual International Mathematical Olympiad (IMO), the Hangzhou-based start-up said on Thursday.
DeepSeek has made widely available its Math-V2 model, which was open-sourced on developer platforms Hugging Face and GitHub, under a permissive licence that allows users to repurpose the model.
Held annually since 1959, the IMO is widely regarded as the world’s most prestigious maths competition, including for AI systems, because of the complicated nature of the questions involved, which require “deep insight, creativity and rigour”, according to Harvard University AI researcher Huang Yichen and Yang Lin, a computer science professor at the University of California, Los Angeles.
To achieve top scores, AI systems must demonstrate the reasoning behind their outputs, rather than just provide simple answers. Around 8 per cent of human IMO participants achieve a gold medal on the test.
DeepSeek’s accomplishment was expected to lower the barrier for developers around the world to access a powerful maths AI model, a few months after leading US AI giants Google DeepMind and OpenAI achieved the same feat at IMO with their proprietary models.
The Chinese firm claimed its model achieved gold-level scores on questions in both this year’s IMO and the 2024 Chinese Mathematical Olympiad.

“Imagine owning the brain of one of the best mathematicians in the world for free to explore it for research, fine-tune it, optimise it and run it on your own hardware,” Hugging Face co-founder and CEO Clement Delangue wrote in a post on X.
“No limitations … no company or government to take it back,” Delangue said. “That’s democratisation of AI and knowledge at its best, literally.”
In a Hugging Face blog post on Thursday, DeepSeek researchers said mathematical capabilities could impact scientific research if further advanced, but many existing AI systems had instead been optimised for strong performance on maths benchmarks, without meaningful improvements in their underlying reasoning capabilities.
By contrast, DeepSeek sought to improve the rigour of its model’s mathematical reasoning abilities by enabling it to “self-verify” answers, even for maths questions without known solutions, according to the researchers.
That addressed a bottleneck of improving current AI systems where they only easily improve on tasks with easily verifiable solutions.
“While much work remains, these results suggest that self-verifiable mathematical reasoning is a feasible research direction that may help develop more capable mathematical AI systems,” the researchers wrote.
Google DeepMind’s IMO gold medal-winning proprietary model was later made available to its premium Ultra plan subscribers. Meanwhile, OpenAI CEO Sam Altman had said the company’s gold medal-winning “experimental” model would not be made available to the public for “many months”.

data.title: New Hong Kong women’s cricket coach happy to sacrifice India life for growth story
data.description:
Mamatha Maben said she wanted to oversee a “growth story” for Hong Kong cricket after upending her life to become head coach of the women’s city team.
The former India international all-rounder, who has previously been in charge of China and Bangladesh, has signed a three-year deal to lead the world No 22 team.
Maben said she had to “think carefully about moving”, with four dogs, two cats and a family to take into consideration.
“But the offer was too good to let go because of the scope of growth I can be involved in,” Maben said. “I sacrificed three years to be part of a growth story.”
Maben arrived in Malaysia on Friday ahead of her debut assignment, a four-match T20 series against the world No 26 hosts, beginning on Sunday.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Cricket Hong Kong, China (@hkcricket)



The 55-year-old does not officially start until Monday, but she packed up her Karnataka home in a hurry for the chance to gain early insight into her new players.
“Matches give you a true indicator of a player’s temperament and skills, so it’s going to give me a good understanding of what I have to work with,” Maben said.
Speaking shortly before her squad joined her in Selangor, Maben said she was “slightly nervous, but quietly excited” about delivering her introductory talk, which she said would focus on the importance of team unity.
“It’s one of the most important elements of any success story,” Maben said. “We need more communication, to understand each other and to all look in the same direction.
“If that base is not established, everything will crumble. It’s a challenge, but it’s non-negotiable for me.”
Maben has set the target of climbing seven places in the world rankings in her first two years, although her initial focus is on Asia Cup qualifiers in February.
In September, there will be the Asian Games in Japan, while the 2030 World Cup, which will be expanded from 12 to 16 teams, is also on her radar. She played in the eight-team edition in 1993.
“It’s going to give opportunities for more nations to taste elite cricket, which broadens your vision and helps you improve,” Maben said. “There’s going to be a lot of funding and effort [from many countries] going into trying to qualify.”
Another attraction of Hong Kong for Maben was the chance to “go somewhere my skill set is needed”.
“Hong Kong is a growing associate nation,” Maben said. “I’m looking forward to this assignment, the team has talent; now it needs direction and purpose.”
Burji Shroff, the chairman of Cricket Hong Kong, China, said Maben’s “impressive track record and commitment to women’s cricket will be invaluable as we strive to enhance our women’s programme”.
“We believe she will inspire our players to reach their full potential and help elevate the profile of women’s cricket in Hong Kong,” Shroff added.

data.title: Why new coach Gavin Lee could be key to Singapore football’s revival: ‘future is bright’
data.description:
Singapore’s long-frustrated football fans are daring to hope again after a landmark Asian Cup qualification win and the appointment of a home-grown coach they believe can restore pride to a national side long eclipsed at home by English Premier League fandom.
On Friday, the Football Association of Singapore (FAS) announced that Gavin Lee, the interim coach that secured the Lions’ qualification for the Asian Cup on merit for the first time, has been given the head coach job on an 18-month contract.
Singapore came from a goal down to beat Hong Kong 2-1 at Kai Tak Stadium on November 18, granting the city state a spot in Saudi Arabia in 2027 and a S$2 million (US$1.54 million) reward, which was announced by FAS President and Chief Executive of Sea Limited Forrest Li.
In the days following the loss, Englishman Ashley Westwood quit as Hong Kong head coach after severe backlash from fans and a public questioning of his tactics by the city’s sports minister Rosanna Law Shuk-pui.

In Singapore, observers and fans alike lauded Lee’s appointment, with football consultant Richard Harcus calling the 35-year-old’s appointment a “long-needed change in Singaporean football”.
Lee is Singapore’s first permanent local head coach since V Sundramoorthy was appointed in 2016.
“Gavin ‘gets’ Singapore, its players, its history, its limitations, its challenges and its desires,” Harcus told This Week in Asia. “Gavin wants the same things too, he has shown he understands already how to go about making this happen.”
Likewise, Singapore’s most successful national coach, Radojko Avramovic, noted that some of the foreign coaches since his tenure concluded in 2012 struggled with understanding the city state’s sporting culture – a problem that Lee will not face.
“They didn’t understand the actual way of life in Singapore and sports. They didn’t really adapt, and I think with Gavin now coming in, for him, this is everyday life,” Avramovic, who won the Asean Championship three times with the Lions, told This Week in Asia in a phone interview.
“Plus, he is a big enthusiast for football and he is learning a lot, this means that the future in front of him is bright.”
The Serb lauded Lee’s ability to blend the old guard leaders of the team with the younger players during the Asian Cup qualifying campaign. The Lions won two and drew one in three games to secure a spot in the tournament that will be held in Saudi Arabia in January and February 2027.

Singapore, which has about 3.66 million citizens, last won a trophy in 2012 – Avramovic’s final Asean Championship – and is currently ranked 151 in the world by Fifa.
The four-time winners clinched the Asean Championship for the first time in 1998 under coach Barry Whitbread.
Lee, a former youth national player, had led Singapore Premier League side BG Tampines Rovers for almost eight years.
He assumed the interim role at the national team after former coach Tsutomu Ogura of Japan resigned in June due to personal reasons. Local media outlet The Straits Times said Lee was handed the role primarily due to his familiarity with the team as Ogura’s assistant.
The local outlet had in July reported that names including World Cup winner Fabio Cannavaro and former Liverpool winger Harry Kewell were shortlisted and two men – Nottingham Forest assistant coach Nick Montgomery and former Iraq coach Jesus Casas – flew into the city state for talks with FAS and Sport Singapore officials.
Asked if Lee needed more experience before taking on the top job, former manager and Asian football pundit Steve Darby said: “The most important thing to me is: has he got the players on his side? It appears he has.”
Harcus argued that Lee’s youth would bring in a new generation of fans and much-needed energy.
“Of course, that brings its challenges, but I’m sure Gavin will welcome that challenge. He has never shied away from a challenge in all the time I’ve known him and that goes back to his youth coaching days in Singapore.”
Avramovic, meanwhile, noted that despite the 18-month contract, Lee needed full support from the association and that he should start thinking about what the team would look like in five years.
“Because Singapore does not have a big choice of players, and they must now really plan and try to build the players what they need for the future,” the former coach said.

For now, observers and fans said there was a feel-good factor among fans that needed to be capitalised on.
Zulkifli Baharudin, former FAS board member, cites Singaporeans’ emotional attachment to the Kallang Roar, a term coined following the 1973 Southeast Asian Peninsular Games to describe the deafening cheers of local fans at the country’s former national stadium.
“Deep inside many Singaporeans, those who are not football fans too, there’s a certain lofty emotional feeling about that Kallang Roar, so when we won this qualifying game for the 2027 Asian Cup and the fact that we were led by a coach who’s a local, it sort of fit into this fairy tale story,” Zulkifli said.
“We have had such a miserable run, and now we have a good moment, so why not let him try and see if we can carry it on for a bit longer.”
Local football fan Akbar Hashim, 64, who runs Singapore Die Hard Fans that organises tours to watch the Lions’ away games, said he had received calls from regular supporters and newcomers on booking their trips to the cup final even though the draw for the tournament and venues for the matches had not been announced.
“This is a good period for Singapore football. With this win, it will only boost the supporters to want to support the team more,” said Akbar, who has been a Lions fan for more than 40 years.
“It’s not an ordinary tournament, it’s a big tournament, so everybody is looking forward to it.”
Lee’s first match as permanent head coach will be at home against Bangladesh on March 31 in the final Asian Cup qualification game. His first major assignment will be the Asean Championship in July and August next year.
Speaking to local media on Friday, Lee said his goals for his tenure would be decided collectively through a conversation with the team.
On the 2027 Asian Cup, he said: “I’m sure the boys want to give the best showing that they can possibly give. And like I’ve said many times before, one of the main intentions is to give the people in Singapore a national team that they can be proud of.”

data.title: Survivor of child trafficking in China becomes multimillionaire; journey inspires public
data.description:
A child trafficking survivor from central China who turned into a multimillionaire has reunited with her birth family, only to discover that her father had died years ago after being consumed by guilt.
On November 20, during the Winter Clothing Festival, mainland media revisited Zeng Youdi’s emotional journey. This festival, alongside Ching Ming Festival and Zhongyuan (Hungry Ghost Festival), is one of China’s three major ghost festivals, during which people burn paper clothes to honour their deceased loved ones.
Zeng, now 46, was abducted at the age of three after being separated from her mother in a crowded market in Hunan province, according to Sohu News.

Though her adoptive parents never abused her, Zeng always felt a disconnect.
Once, she tried to run away while they were asleep, but her adoptive father caught her and scolded her for hours.
In primary school, some classmates called her “the picked-up child,” a taunt that wounded her self-esteem deeply.
Eager to earn her adoptive parents’ love, Zeng threw herself into farm work, learning to cut pigweed after school.

She carefully nurtured her bond with them, and they would occasionally praise her as a “good daughter.”
At 15, Zeng was sent to Shenzhen to live with her adoptive parents’ relatives and work as a nanny.
By 18, she left that life behind and took a bold step to work as a saleswoman at an electronics company in Shenzhen, despite not having a university degree.
Determined to change her fate, Zeng made hundreds of calls daily and travelled to meet clients across the region. Her hard work paid off when she was promoted to vice-president of sales and soon launched her own electronics company.

When she had made enough money, Zeng bought a car and a house in Shenzhen, while her company expanded with multiple stores, raking in over 70 million yuan (US$10 million) annually.
In 2019, she appeared on a mainland TV show in search of her biological parents.
With the help of the show’s team, Zeng uncovered the truth about her separation from her mother. On that fateful day, her mother, who was a psychiatric patient, had fainted at the market, and when she woke up, Zeng was gone.
Her father had given up everything to search for her, but the emotional strain took its toll, and he eventually died from lung disease.

On the show, Zeng met her biological siblings and embraced them. Tearfully, she said: “I wasn’t abandoned. I finally have a home!”
She later returned to Hunan to visit her mother, who, despite her mental illness, remembered Zeng’s favourite childhood candy and had bought some for her.
Zeng currently cares for her mother alongside her siblings while continuing to run her electronics business. Each year, she visits her father’s grave.
Her remarkable journey home has touched many on mainland social media.
One online observer commented: “Zeng survived being trafficked and redeemed herself through intelligence and hard work. Fate was both her enemy and greatest teacher, shaping her into the strong woman she is today.”

data.title: Hong Kong construction clampdown needed after fire but industry must act too: experts
data.description:
Systemic failings around supervision of building maintenance in Hong Kong, and a lack of coordination between departments charged with ensuring it is done properly, both needed to be addressed to prevent tragedies such as the Tai Po fire happening again, experts and sector representatives have said.
The regulation of polystyrene foam boards was particularly important, they added, with the preliminary investigation into the deadly blaze at Wang Fuk Court finding that the fire started on protective nets covering scaffolding and was intensified by styrofoam, despite the nets meeting fire safety standards.
Officials have launched citywide inspections of buildings under construction or undergoing maintenance, requiring reports within seven days on the fire-retardant performance of materials being used.
And on Friday, the government said the performance of the nets at Wang Fuk Court had been tested and was not found to have aided combustion.
But the Housing Bureau, which conducted the test, said the boards in Wang Chi House, the only block unaffected by the fire, were found to be flammable.

The Buildings Department said that as of 5pm on Friday it had inspected 127 buildings undergoing maintenance to their facades, and had sent samples of scaffolding nets from those sites to be tested.
Two of the buildings checked were found to have styrofoam covering the windows. Officials had ordered they be removed and legal action was being considered.
A construction sector insider, who declined to be named, said polystyrene foam boards were sometimes used in renovation to protect windows from being damaged, but added it should only be applied a few floors at a time and removed immediately after the work was completed.
The source said the contractor might have chosen to use foam, which is cheaper than other protection materials to control costs. Placing foam boards in all eight blocks at once could allow them to collect payment for this procedure first.
It was common practice for contractors to be paid after a job was completed, the source added.
Vincent Ho Kui-yip, the president of the Hong Kong Institute of Building Safety, said while there were rules governing the fire-retardant performance of protective nets, none existed for polystyrene foam boards or styrofoam.
He suggested the cost of some fire-retardant styrofoam products may cause contractors to not use them.
Ho urged authorities to look into regulating the material, among other high-risk procedures identified in the citywide inspection, as it was impractical to regulate every kind of work or material.
He also urged officials to speed up the inspection and testing of the protective nets across the city, and said the Buildings Department currently exempted most maintenance projects from seeking approval before work began.
It only required the submission of documents after the task was finished.
“It is time to standardise the procedures for large-scale maintenance involving an entire building or several buildings,” Ho said. “They should at least submit records of key procedures for the authorities to keep track of them. Authorities will then be able to hold them liable, and it can deter the sector [from cutting corners].”
Professor Yau Yung from Lingnan University, a professional member of Britain’s Royal Institution of Chartered Surveyors, agreed that the government should fill the gap in its code of practice on the use of styrofoam, while closing other loopholes that existed.
“If the government is willing to step up inspection, prosecute those who contravene the law in a high-profile manner to warn the sector, the sector will naturally be more disciplined,” he said.
Yau also said the government should streamline its procedures for dealing with complaints, given the Buildings Department focused on structural safety and the Labour Department on occupational safety.
The answer, Yau said, would be for one “centralised body to deal with the complaints”, and increased public education.
In the case of Wang Fuk Court, the Labour Department received complaints in September concerning the scaffolding nets, something it said it was actively following up.
Lawrence Ng San-wa, permanent honorary president of the Hong Kong Construction Sub-Contractors Association, said the industry should take some responsibility because it was impractical for the government to supervise every detail of the 1,300 projects happening in the city every day.
Ng also said he believed the problem lay in the polystyrene foam boards rather than the safety nets.
“Although flame-retardant nets are more expensive by percentage, the sum is not big after all. Everyone understands that once it catches fire, the loss would only be bigger,” he said.
“As the polyfoam helped conduct heat, the window broke, and the fire entered the units. If there is no polyfoam, the damage should not be that serious,” he added.

data.title: Airbus issues major A320 recall, threatening global flight disruption
data.description:
Europe’s Airbus said on Friday it was ordering immediate repairs to 6,000 of its widely used A320 family of jets in a sweeping recall affecting more than half the global fleet, threatening upheaval during the busiest travel weekend of the year in the United States and disruption worldwide.
The setback appears to be among the largest recalls affecting Airbus in its 55-year history and comes weeks after the A320 overtook the Boeing 737 as the most delivered model. At the time Airbus issued its bulletin to the plane’s more than 350 operators, some 3,000 A320-family jets were in the air.
The fix mainly involves reverting to earlier software and is relatively simple, but must be carried out before the planes can fly again, other than repositioning to repair centres, according to the bulletin to airlines seen by Reuters.
Airlines from the United States to South America, Europe, India and New Zealand warned the repairs could potentially cause flight delays or cancellations.
The world’s largest A320 operator, American Airlines , said some 340 of its 480 A320 aircraft would need the fix. It said it mostly expected these to be completed by Saturday with about two hours required for each plane.
Other airlines said they would take planes briefly out of service to do the repairs, including Germany’s Lufthansa, India’s IndiGo and UK-based easyJet.
Colombian carrier Avianca said the recall affected more than 70 per cent of its fleet, prompting it to close ticket sales for travel dates through December 8.

There are around 11,300 A320-family jets in operation, including 6,440 of the core A320 model, which first flew in 1987.
Four of the world’s 10 biggest A320-family operators are major US airlines: American Airlines, Delta Air Lines, JetBlue and United Airlines. Chinese, European and Indian carriers are also among the jet’s biggest customers.
For about two-thirds of the affected jets, the recall will result in a brief grounding as airlines revert to a previous software version, industry sources said.
Still, that comes at a time when airline repair shops are already overrun by maintenance work, as hundreds of Airbus jets have been grounded due to long waiting times for separate engine repairs or inspections. The industry also has labour shortages.
“The timing is definitely not ideal for an issue like this to arise on one of the most ubiquitous aircraft around the [US] holidays,” Mike Stengel of AeroDynamic Advisory said.
But due to the quick repair time, many jets can be fixed between scheduled flights or during overnight checks, he added.
Britain’s easyJet said it had already completed the work.
A senior airline industry source, however, said sequencing the repairs at a time when demand is high and fleets are already facing maintenance delays was expected to be a major challenge.
Aviation analyst Rob Morris said there were questions over how much hangar capacity would immediately be available.
Airbus said a recent incident had revealed that solar flares may corrupt data critical to the functioning of flight controls.
Industry sources said the incident that triggered the unexpected repair action involved a JetBlue flight from Cancun, Mexico, to Newark, New Jersey, on October 30, in which several passengers were hurt following a sharp loss of altitude.
That A320 jet made an emergency landing at Tampa, Florida, after a flight control problem and a sudden uncommanded drop in altitude, prompting a Federal Aviation Administration investigation. JetBlue and the FAA had no comment on the recall.

The European Union Aviation Safety Agency late on Friday issued an emergency directive making the fix mandatory.
An Airbus spokesperson estimated the repairs would affect 6,000 jets in total.
The temporary groundings for repairs for some airlines could be much longer since more than 1,000 of the affected jets may also have to have hardware changed, industry sources said.
The abrupt recall sent ripples around the world.
A Finnair flight was delayed almost an hour as pilots established which software version they had, a passenger said. Air New Zealand warned of a number of cancellations.
Air France said it was cancelling 35 flights, 5 per cent of the airline’s daily total. Mexico’s Volaris said it would be hit by delays or cancellations for up to 72 hours.
Launched in 1984, the A320 was the first mainstream aircraft to introduce “fly-by-wire” computer controls.
It competes with the Boeing 737 Max, which suffered a lengthy worldwide grounding after fatal crashes in 2018 and 2019, blamed on poorly designed flight control software.
Demand for the two main brands of workhorse jets has surged in recent years as economic growth led by Asia brought tens of millions of new travellers into the skies.
Originally designed to serve hubs, the single-aisle models were later widely adopted by low-cost carriers. The connections they provide now represent a significant slice of the economy.
The Airbus bulletin traced the problem to a flight system called ELAC (Elevator and Aileron Computer), which sends commands from the pilot’s sidestick to elevators at the rear. These in turn control the aircraft’s pitch or nose angle.
The computer’s manufacturer, France’s Thales, said in response to a query that the computer complies with Airbus specifications and the functionality in question is supported by software that is not under Thales’ responsibility.
